page: Vultr voc-m-24c-192gb-4800s-amd is an Optimized Cloud Compute server configured with 24 vCPUs and 192.0 GiB of system memory, yielding an 8.0 GiB memory-to-core ratio. The instance is powered by an AMD EPYC-Rome processor operating at a base frequency of 2.0 GHz on the x86_64 architecture. It features 24 physical cores with a shared CPU allocation model and 1.0 thread per core. Local storage is bundled, consisting of a 4800 GB NVMe SSD, and the configuration includes one complimentary public IPv4 address.

Performance benchmarks indicate strong multi-core capabilities, with stress-ng multi-core and Passmark CPU scores placing in the top 25%. Large-block memory bandwidth is highly efficient, with read-only speeds of 488.87 GB/sec and write speeds of 225.61 GB/sec, both ranking in the top 10%. However, single-core execution and database operations, such as Redis SET operations at 55.54 million operations per second, perform in the average tier. The shared CPU allocation model represents a potential tradeoff for workloads requiring sustained, isolated CPU performance.

This hardware profile offers high resource density, combining substantial memory capacity and large local NVMe storage without the cost overhead of dedicated CPU cores. The server is suited for memory-intensive workloads, large-scale caching layers, data decompression tasks, and medium-scale LLM inference where high memory bandwidth and large storage capacity are required.
description: Vultr voc-m-24c-192gb-4800s-amd is an Optimized Cloud Compute server featuring 24 vCPUs on a shared AMD EPYC-Rome processor running at 2.0 GHz. It is equipped with 192.0 GB of RAM, providing an 8.0 GB per core ratio, and includes a 4800 GB NVMe SSD. Benchmarks show strong multi-core CPU performance and top-tier large-block memory bandwidth, with read speeds reaching 488.87 GB/sec. However, single-core metrics and database operations remain in the average tier. The shared CPU allocation presents a tradeoff for sustained compute-heavy tasks, but the high memory and storage density make it a cost-efficient option for memory-intensive workloads, data decompression, and caching.
